category_title: Low-attaining students who engage through 'playful' drama rather than traditional 'work'
minimal_context: The student is working in a collaborative group in the English classroom and is asked to practice a drama performance.
dialogue:
TUTOR: Alright, it is time for your group to stand up and practice your drama performance for the rest of the class.

STUDENT: I don't want to do the drama activity.

TUTOR: You need to practice with your group.

STUDENT: I just don’t like performing in front of people.

TUTOR: It is part of the lesson, so please stand up and try.

STUDENT: No, some people judge you. I'm not doing the performance.

options.A: Transition the decision as to when, and for how long, to take quiet time away from the collaborative group work from the teacher to the student.
options.B: Encourage students to assume leadership roles and enable groups to assign these themselves, offering a range of roles (lead director, lead script editor, lead creative designer, lead marketing representative).
options.C: Respond to the student's actions, utterances, and intentions during the collaborative group work and drama activities in a consistently responsive and contingent manner.
options.D: Place special emphasis on the developmental integration of affect, the vocabulary of emotion, and cognitive understanding during the collaborative group work and drama activities.
